QRM.question: Are you being interfered with?
QRM.notes: Refers to man-made interference from other stations.

QRN.meaning: I am troubled by static noise
QRN.question: Are you troubled by static noise?
QRN.notes: Refers to natural static, e.g. from thunderstorms.

QRP.question: Shall I reduce transmitter power?
QRP.notes: Also used to describe low power operation (typically 5W or less).

QRT.question: Shall I stop sending?
QRT.notes: Used when a station is going off air.

QRV.question: Are you ready?
QRV.notes: Used to indicate readiness to receive transmissions.

QRZ.question: Who is calling me?
QRZ.notes: Also the name of a popular amateur radio callsign lookup website.

QSB.question: Are my signals fading?
QSB.notes: Fading is common on HF due to ionospheric changes.

QSL.question: Can you acknowledge receipt?
QSL.notes: Also refers to QSL cards — confirmation cards sent between operators.

QSO.question: Can you communicate with ... direct?
QSO.notes: A QSO is a two-way amateur radio contact.

QSY.question: Shall I change to another frequency?
QSY.notes: Used to move off a calling frequency after making contact.

QTH.question: What is your location?
QTH.notes: Often given as a town, Maidenhead locator, or grid square.

QSX.question: Will you listen on ... kHz?
QSX.notes: Used when a station is listening on a different frequency to transmitting.

QRX.meaning: Wait / I will call you again at ...
QRX.question: Shall I standby?
QRX.notes: Used to ask a station to wait before transmitting.

QTR.question: What is the exact time?
QTR.notes: Used to give or request the current time.

QSP.question: Will you relay to ...?
QSP.notes: Used when passing a message through an intermediary station.

QRK.question: What is the readability of my signals?
QRK.notes: Part of the RST reporting system — R stands for readability (1-5).

QSA.question: What is the strength of my signals?
QSA.notes: Part of the RST reporting system — S stands for signal strength (1-9).
